name: Toots Mondello
abstract: Nunzio (Toots) Mondello (August 14, 1911 – November 15, 1992) was an American swing jazz alto saxophonist. Born in Boston, Massachusetts, Mondello played with Mal Hallett from 1927 to 1933, where he also simultaneously performed saxophone and trombone, and with Irving Aaronson's Commanders, Joe Haymes, and Buddy Rogers.

concerts.label: Paul Whiteman and His All American Band
JVC Jazz Festival: Salute to Benny Goodman

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Conniff
about_quotes.evidence: And the guys that played with Benny’s band, Hymie Schertzer and Toots Mondello were playing the alto saxophone
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Conniff interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 14, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Loren Schoenberg
about_quotes.evidence: Toots Mondello is mentioned only as one of the musicians Dick Hyman was going to put together for a TV show ('Hymie Shertzer , Toots Mondello, and all the guys that were still alive'), but the interviewee (Loren Schoenberg) does not claim to have met, played with, or worked with him directly.
about_quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019

about_quotes.speaker: Maxine Sullivan
about_quotes.evidence: But looking at this line-up here, we had some pretty heavies here. Like Manny Klein, Charlie Spivack, Jack Lacey, Toots Mondello, Jess Carneol, Babe Russin.

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Whaley
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ...and there's -- Toots Mondello, all from the Boston area. [INTERVIEWEE] Sure, Boston was a music culture place there.

about_quotes.speaker: Bud Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: Toots Mondello, the great -- now, he has become quite a composer, having studied some 20 years with Creston, I believe.

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Carter
about_quotes.evidence: That's when I first met Toots. And we struck up and maintained a very nice association through the years.
